home.faq_q1: What is a dynamic QR code?
home.faq_a1: A dynamic QR code lets you change the content without reprinting the code itself. Perfect for menus, marketing campaigns, or any evolving content.
home.faq_q2: What is the difference between Free and Pro?
home.faq_a2: The Free plan gives you QR code creation with basic analytics. The Pro plan unlocks full analytics, PDF/ZIP export, permanent QR codes and priority support.
home.faq_q3: Can I cancel my Pro subscription?
home.faq_a3: Yes, at any time from the Stripe customer portal. No commitment, no cancellation fees.
home.faq_a4: On the Free plan, QR codes are valid for 30 days. On the Pro plan, they are permanent and never expire.
home.faq_a5: PNG and JPEG for all plans, vector PDF and bulk ZIP for the Pro plan.
home.faq_1_a: Yes, useqraft is 100% free. You can create unlimited QR codes, customize them with your colors and logo, and export them in PNG, JPEG or PDF — no fees, no credit card.
home.faq_2_q: Can I add my logo to a QR code?
home.faq_2_a: Yes. useqraft lets you embed your logo at the center of your QR code for complete brand customization. The QR remains readable thanks to the high error correction level.
home.faq_3_a: useqraft offers three formats: PNG for the web, JPEG for standard printing, and vector PDF for perfect quality at any size.
home.faq_4_q: Can I track scans of my QR codes?
home.faq_4_a: Yes. Each QR code has complete statistics: total scan count, trends over time, breakdown by device, browser and OS, plus a geographic scan map.
home.pro_cta_subtitle: Advanced analytics · Permanent QR codes · Unlimited export · Priority support

faq.q2: Does the QR code store my password?
faq.a2: The password is encoded directly in the QR code image. It is not stored on any server — everything stays on your device.
faq.q3: Will it work on iPhone and Android?
faq.a3: Yes. Both iOS (11+) and Android natively support WiFi QR codes through their camera app.
faq.q4: Can I use it for my restaurant or hotel?

faq.q1: What is a vCard QR code?

faq.q2: Does it work on all phones?
faq.a2: Yes. All modern smartphones (iOS and Android) support vCard QR codes through their built-in camera app.
faq.a3: The standard vCard QR code does not include photos as it would make the QR too complex to scan reliably. Use a Pro account to add a logo.

faq.q5: Can I update my details after printing?

faq.title: FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
faq.q1: Why use a QR code for my restaurant menu?
faq.a1: It's hygienic (contactless), saves printing costs, and lets you update your menu anytime without reprinting.
faq.q2: Do customers need an app to scan?
faq.a2: No. All modern smartphones can scan QR codes directly from the camera app. No special app needed.
faq.q3: Can I link to a PDF menu?
faq.a3: Yes. Upload your PDF anywhere (Google Drive, your website) and use that link. The QR code will open it directly.
faq.q4: What if I change my menu?
faq.a4: If you keep the same URL, just update the content at that link. With a Pro account, you can also change the destination URL without reprinting.
faq.q5: Can I track how many people scan it?
faq.a5: Yes! Create a free useqraft account to get scan analytics: number of scans, device types, location, and more.

faq.title: FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
faq.q1: What happens when someone scans it?
faq.a1: Their default email app opens with the recipient, subject, and body already filled in. They just hit Send.
faq.q2: Does it work with Gmail, Outlook, etc.?
faq.a2: Yes. The QR code uses the standard mailto: protocol which works with any email client.
faq.q3: Can I track how many emails are sent?
faq.a3: The QR code opens the user's email app, so we can't track sends. With a Pro account, you can track scans though.
faq.q4: Is the email address visible in the QR?
faq.a4: The email is encoded in the QR code data. It's not visible by looking at the code, but any QR reader will decode it.
faq.q5: Can I add CC or BCC recipients?
faq.a5: The standard mailto: format supports CC and BCC, but for simplicity we focus on the primary recipient. You can manually edit the generated URL.

faq.a1: The QR code uses the geo: URI scheme. On Android, it opens Google Maps. On iOS, it opens Apple Maps by default.
faq.q2: How do I find my coordinates?
faq.a2: Open Google Maps, right-click your location, and click the coordinates to copy them.
faq.q3: Can I add a pin label?
faq.a3: Yes. The label field adds a query parameter that shows a named pin on the map.
faq.a4: The QR code encodes coordinates locally, but the user needs an internet connection to load the map.
faq.q5: Can I use an address instead of coordinates?
faq.a5: For best accuracy, use coordinates. However, you can use the label field with a full address and it will search for it.

faq.q1: Where should I host my PDF?

faq.q1: How much text can fit in a QR code?
faq.a1: Up to about 4,296 characters, but for reliable scanning, keep it under 300 characters. Shorter is better.
faq.q2: Can the text include special characters?
faq.a2: Yes. QR codes support the full UTF-8 character set: accents, symbols, emojis, CJK characters.
faq.q3: What happens when someone scans it?
faq.a3: The text is displayed on screen. If it contains a URL, most scanners offer to open it. Plain text is just shown.
faq.q4: Can I use it for coupon codes?
faq.a4: Absolutely. Encode discount codes, promo codes, or serial numbers that your POS system can scan and validate.
faq.q5: Is there a difference between text and URL QR codes?
faq.a5: A URL QR code auto-opens the link. A text QR code just displays the content. Use text for non-URL data.

fitness.faq_q3: How do yoga studios use QR codes?
fitness.faq_a3: Studios use QR codes for class schedules, booking, WiFi access and collecting reviews from students.
fitness.faq_a4: Yes. useqraft is 100% free with no signup required.

glossary.hub_subtitle: Everything you need to know about QR codes, explained simply.
glossary.meta_title_hub: QR Code Glossary — Key Terms Explained | useqraft
glossary.meta_description_hub: Learn every QR code term: dynamic, static, error correction, vCard, analytics, and more. Clear definitions with examples.
glossary.cta_body: Put this knowledge into practice — generate your first QR code in seconds.

dynamic_qr_code.title: Dynamic QR Code
dynamic_qr_code.meta_title: What is a Dynamic QR Code? | useqraft Glossary
dynamic_qr_code.meta_description: A dynamic QR code uses a short redirect URL, allowing you to change the destination without reprinting.
dynamic_qr_code.definition: A dynamic QR code encodes a short redirect URL instead of the final destination. This means you can update where the QR code points without reprinting it. Scans are routed through a tracking server that records analytics.
dynamic_qr_code.how_it_works: When scanned, the QR code opens a short URL hosted on a redirect server. The server logs the scan (time, location, device) then redirects the user to the actual destination. You can change this destination at any time from your dashboard without modifying the printed QR code.

example_1.title: Restaurant menu updates
example_1.description: Change your menu URL seasonally without reprinting table QR codes.

example_2.title: Marketing campaigns
example_2.description: A/B test landing pages by switching the destination URL mid-campaign.

example_3.title: Event tickets
example_3.description: Update event details after tickets are printed.

dynamic_qr_code.faq_q1: Can I convert a static QR code to dynamic?
dynamic_qr_code.faq_a1: No. The encoding is different. You need to create a new dynamic QR code from the start.
dynamic_qr_code.faq_a2: It depends on the provider. On useqraft, free QR codes expire after 30 days. Pro QR codes are permanent.
dynamic_qr_code.faq_q3: Are dynamic QR codes slower to scan?
dynamic_qr_code.faq_a3: The redirect adds a few milliseconds, but it is imperceptible to users.

static_qr_code.title: Static QR Code
static_qr_code.meta_title: What is a Static QR Code? | useqraft Glossary
static_qr_code.meta_description: A static QR code encodes data directly. It never expires but cannot be edited after creation.
static_qr_code.definition: A static QR code embeds the data (URL, text, WiFi credentials) directly into its pattern. Once generated, the content cannot be changed. It works offline and never expires.
static_qr_code.how_it_works: The data is encoded directly into the QR code's matrix of black and white modules. When scanned, the device reads the data without needing any server. This makes static codes completely self-contained and permanent.

example_1.title: WiFi sharing
example_1.description: Print a QR code with your WiFi credentials — it works forever without any server.

example_2.title: Business cards
example_2.description: Encode your vCard contact info directly into a QR code on your card.

example_3.title: Product labels
example_3.description: Link to a fixed product page that won't change.

static_qr_code.faq_q1: Can I track scans on a static QR code?
static_qr_code.faq_a1: Not directly. Static codes don't route through a server, so there's no way to count scans.
static_qr_code.faq_q2: What happens if I need to change the URL?
static_qr_code.faq_a2: You must generate a new QR code with the updated URL and replace the printed one.
static_qr_code.faq_q3: Is there a size limit for static QR codes?
static_qr_code.faq_a3: Yes. The more data you encode, the denser the pattern becomes, making it harder to scan. Keep URLs short.

error_correction.title: Error Correction
error_correction.meta_title: QR Code Error Correction Levels Explained | useqraft Glossary
error_correction.meta_description: QR codes use Reed-Solomon error correction (L, M, Q, H) to remain scannable even when partially damaged.
error_correction.definition: Error correction allows a QR code to be scanned even if part of it is damaged or obscured. QR codes use Reed-Solomon error correction with four levels: L (7%), M (15%), Q (25%), and H (30%).
error_correction.how_it_works: Reed-Solomon codes add redundant data modules to the QR pattern. If some modules are unreadable (due to dirt, scratches, or a logo overlay), the decoder can reconstruct the missing data. Higher levels mean more redundancy but also a denser (larger) QR code.

example_1.title: Logo overlay
example_1.description: Use level H to safely place a logo in the center of your QR code.

example_2.title: Outdoor signage
example_2.description: Use level Q or H for codes exposed to weather and wear.

example_3.title: Digital screens
example_3.description: Level L is sufficient for clean digital displays.

error_correction.faq_q1: Which error correction level should I use?
error_correction.faq_a1: Use M for most cases. Use H if you're adding a logo. Use L only for very long URLs where size matters.
error_correction.faq_q2: Does higher error correction make the QR code bigger?
error_correction.faq_a2: Yes. More redundancy means more modules, resulting in a denser pattern that needs to be printed larger.
error_correction.faq_q3: Can I change the error correction level after creation?
error_correction.faq_a3: No. Changing the level regenerates an entirely different QR code pattern.

qr_code_vs_barcode.title: QR Code vs Barcode
qr_code_vs_barcode.meta_title: QR Code vs Barcode: Key Differences | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_vs_barcode.meta_description: QR codes store data in 2D (thousands of characters) while barcodes are 1D (up to 20 characters). Compare features and use cases.
qr_code_vs_barcode.definition: A barcode is a one-dimensional (1D) pattern of vertical lines that encodes up to ~20 characters. A QR code is a two-dimensional (2D) matrix that can store up to 4,296 alphanumeric characters.
qr_code_vs_barcode.how_it_works: Barcodes encode data in the width and spacing of parallel lines, read by a laser scanner in one direction. QR codes use a grid of dark and light modules read in two dimensions by a camera, enabling vastly more data capacity and built-in error correction.

example_1.title: Product inventory
example_1.description: Barcodes are standard for SKU scanning in retail and warehouses.

example_2.title: Marketing materials
example_2.description: QR codes link to websites, videos, or forms — impossible with barcodes.

example_3.title: Boarding passes
example_3.description: Airlines use QR codes to encode passenger data, gate, and seat info.

qr_code_vs_barcode.faq_a1: Not entirely. Barcodes remain dominant in retail POS. QR codes excel in marketing, payments, and consumer engagement.
qr_code_vs_barcode.faq_a2: Yes, but dedicated apps are often needed. QR codes work natively with most phone cameras.
qr_code_vs_barcode.faq_a3: Neither is inherently secure — both encode plain data. Security depends on what the data links to.

qr_code_size.title: QR Code Size
qr_code_size.meta_title: QR Code Size Guide: Minimum Dimensions | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_size.meta_description: Learn the minimum QR code size for reliable scanning. Rule of thumb: 1/10 of the scanning distance.
qr_code_size.definition: QR code size refers to the physical dimensions of the printed or displayed code. The minimum size depends on scanning distance, data density, and error correction level.
qr_code_size.how_it_works: The general rule is that the QR code should be at least 1/10 of the scanning distance. For a phone held 30cm away, the QR code needs to be at least 3cm. More data or higher error correction increases the number of modules, requiring a larger print size.

example_1.title: Business card
example_1.description: Minimum 2cm × 2cm for close-range scanning.

example_2.title: Poster or flyer
example_2.description: At least 3-5cm for arm's length scanning.

example_3.title: Billboard
example_3.description: 15cm+ for scanning from several meters away.

qr_code_size.faq_q1: What is the minimum QR code size?
qr_code_size.faq_a1: 2cm × 2cm for close-range scanning (under 30cm). Scale up proportionally for greater distances.
qr_code_size.faq_q2: Does the amount of data affect size?
qr_code_size.faq_a2: Yes. More data means more modules, requiring a larger physical size to keep each module scannable.
qr_code_size.faq_q3: Can a QR code be too large?
qr_code_size.faq_a3: Technically no, but very large codes on small screens may require the user to step back to scan.

qr_code_resolution.title: QR Code Resolution
qr_code_resolution.meta_title: QR Code Resolution: DPI and Export Guide | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_resolution.meta_description: Export QR codes at 300 DPI minimum for print. Use SVG for infinite scalability.
qr_code_resolution.definition: Resolution refers to the clarity and detail of a QR code image, measured in DPI (dots per inch) for raster formats or as vector for SVG.
qr_code_resolution.how_it_works: Raster formats (PNG, JPEG) have a fixed pixel grid. At low resolution, individual modules become blurry and unscannable. Vector formats (SVG) use mathematical shapes and scale infinitely without quality loss. For print, always use 300 DPI minimum or SVG.

example_1.title: Print materials
example_1.description: Export at 300+ DPI in PNG or use SVG for perfect print quality at any size.

example_2.title: Web display
example_2.description: 72-150 DPI is sufficient for screens.

example_3.title: Large format printing
example_3.description: SVG is ideal — scales from business card to billboard.

qr_code_resolution.faq_q1: What resolution should I use for printing?
qr_code_resolution.faq_a1: 300 DPI minimum. For large format printing, use SVG to avoid pixelation.
qr_code_resolution.faq_q2: What is the difference between PNG and SVG?
qr_code_resolution.faq_a2: PNG is a raster format with fixed pixels. SVG is a vector format that scales infinitely without quality loss.
qr_code_resolution.faq_q3: Can I upscale a low-resolution QR code?
qr_code_resolution.faq_a3: Upscaling adds blur. Regenerate the QR code at the correct resolution instead.

vcard_qr_code.title: vCard QR Code
vcard_qr_code.meta_title: What is a vCard QR Code? | useqraft Glossary
vcard_qr_code.meta_description: A vCard QR code encodes contact information (name, phone, email, address) that can be saved directly to a phone.
vcard_qr_code.definition: A vCard QR code encodes contact details in the vCard format. When scanned, the phone prompts the user to save the contact directly to their address book.
vcard_qr_code.how_it_works: The QR code contains structured text in vCard format (BEGIN:VCARD ... END:VCARD) with fields for name, phone, email, company, title, address, and website. The phone's OS recognizes this format and offers to create a new contact.

example_1.title: Business networking
example_1.description: Share your contact info instantly at conferences without paper cards.

example_2.title: Employee badges
example_2.description: Print on ID badges so visitors can save contact details.

example_3.title: Real estate signs
example_3.description: Let potential buyers save the agent's contact from the for-sale sign.

vcard_qr_code.faq_q1: How many fields can a vCard QR code hold?
vcard_qr_code.faq_a1: Standard fields include name, phone, email, company, title, address, website, and notes. Adding too many fields makes the QR code denser.
vcard_qr_code.faq_q2: Does it work on all phones?
vcard_qr_code.faq_a2: Yes. Both iOS and Android natively recognize vCard format and offer to save the contact.
vcard_qr_code.faq_q3: Can I include a profile photo?
vcard_qr_code.faq_a3: Technically yes, but base64-encoded images massively increase QR code density. Use a URL field linking to your profile instead.

wifi_qr_code.title: WiFi QR Code
wifi_qr_code.meta_title: What is a WiFi QR Code? | useqraft Glossary
wifi_qr_code.meta_description: A WiFi QR code lets users connect to a network instantly by scanning — no typing passwords.
wifi_qr_code.definition: A WiFi QR code encodes network credentials (SSID, password, encryption type) so that scanning it automatically connects the device to the WiFi network.
wifi_qr_code.how_it_works: The QR code contains a special string format: WIFI:T:WPA;S:NetworkName;P:Password;;. When scanned, the phone reads this format and offers to connect to the network automatically, without the user typing anything.

example_1.title: Guest WiFi at home
example_1.description: Print and frame near your router so guests connect instantly.

example_2.title: Hotel rooms
example_2.description: Place on the nightstand or room card for instant WiFi access.

example_3.title: Coworking spaces
example_3.description: Display at the entrance so members connect without asking staff.

wifi_qr_code.faq_q1: Does it work if the password changes?
wifi_qr_code.faq_a1: No. You need to generate a new QR code with the updated password. Use a dynamic QR if you change passwords frequently.
wifi_qr_code.faq_q2: Is it secure to share WiFi via QR code?
wifi_qr_code.faq_a2: It's as secure as sharing the password verbally. The QR code just automates the typing. Use WPA2/WPA3 encryption.
wifi_qr_code.faq_q3: Does it work on both iPhone and Android?
wifi_qr_code.faq_a3: Yes. iOS 11+ and Android 10+ support WiFi QR code scanning natively.

scan_analytics.title: Scan Analytics
scan_analytics.meta_title: QR Code Scan Analytics Explained | useqraft Glossary
scan_analytics.meta_description: Track QR code scans: total count, location, device, browser, OS, and timeline. Available with dynamic QR codes.
scan_analytics.definition: Scan analytics is the data collected each time a dynamic QR code is scanned: timestamp, geographic location, device type, browser, and operating system.
scan_analytics.how_it_works: When a dynamic QR code is scanned, the redirect server logs metadata from the HTTP request: IP-based geolocation, User-Agent string (parsed for device, browser, OS), and timestamp. This data is aggregated into dashboards showing scan trends over time, geographic distribution, and device breakdown.

example_1.title: Marketing ROI
example_1.description: Track how many people scanned your flyer QR code and from which cities.

example_2.title: Event engagement
example_2.description: See real-time scan counts during an event to gauge interest.

example_3.title: A/B testing
example_3.description: Compare scan rates between two poster designs placed in different locations.

scan_analytics.faq_q1: Can I see who scanned my QR code?
scan_analytics.faq_a1: No. Analytics are anonymous — you see aggregated data (city, device type) but not personal identity.
scan_analytics.faq_q2: Do static QR codes have analytics?
scan_analytics.faq_a2: No. Only dynamic QR codes route through a server that can log scans.
scan_analytics.faq_a3: City-level accuracy using IP geolocation. It's not GPS-precise but sufficient for marketing insights.

qr_code_tracking.title: QR Code Tracking
qr_code_tracking.meta_title: QR Code Tracking: How to Monitor Scans | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_tracking.meta_description: QR code tracking records each scan with location, device, and time data via a redirect server.
qr_code_tracking.definition: QR code tracking refers to monitoring scan activity on dynamic QR codes. Each scan is logged with metadata including location, device, browser, OS, and timestamp.
qr_code_tracking.how_it_works: Dynamic QR codes point to a redirect URL. When scanned, the server processes the request, extracts metadata from the IP address and User-Agent header, logs it to a database, then redirects to the final destination. The tracking is invisible to the user.

example_1.title: Campaign attribution
example_1.description: Track which physical materials drive the most digital engagement.

example_2.title: Geographic insights
example_2.description: Discover which cities or countries your audience scans from.

example_3.title: Time-based trends
example_3.description: Identify peak scanning hours to optimize your campaign schedule.

qr_code_tracking.faq_q1: Is QR code tracking GDPR compliant?
qr_code_tracking.faq_a1: useqraft tracks anonymized data only (city-level location, device type). No personal data or cookies are used for scan tracking.
qr_code_tracking.faq_a2: Yes. useqraft lets you export scan data as CSV or view it in the analytics dashboard.
qr_code_tracking.faq_q3: What's the difference between tracking and analytics?
qr_code_tracking.faq_a3: Tracking is the data collection process. Analytics is the visualization and interpretation of that data.

qr_code_expiration.title: QR Code Expiration
qr_code_expiration.meta_title: Do QR Codes Expire? | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_expiration.meta_description: Static QR codes never expire. Dynamic QR codes may expire depending on your plan. Learn how expiration works.
qr_code_expiration.definition: QR code expiration depends on the type. Static QR codes never expire because data is embedded directly. Dynamic QR codes can expire if the redirect service stops or the subscription lapses.
qr_code_expiration.how_it_works: Static codes are permanent — the data is in the pattern itself. Dynamic codes depend on the redirect server being active. If the server URL stops responding (subscription ends, service shuts down), the QR code becomes non-functional. On useqraft, free QR codes expire after 30 days; Pro QR codes are permanent.

example_1.title: Temporary promotions
example_1.description: Set expiration dates for limited-time offers to prevent misuse.

example_2.title: Event tickets
example_2.description: QR codes that auto-expire after the event date.

example_3.title: Permanent signage
example_3.description: Use Pro plan for codes on permanent fixtures like plaques or signs.

qr_code_expiration.faq_q1: Can I extend an expired QR code?
qr_code_expiration.faq_a1: On useqraft, upgrading to Pro reactivates expired QR codes within the 7-day grace period.
qr_code_expiration.faq_a2: Static printed codes never expire. Dynamic printed codes expire if the redirect service stops.
qr_code_expiration.faq_q3: How do I make a QR code permanent?
qr_code_expiration.faq_a3: Use a static QR code (permanent by nature) or a Pro dynamic QR code on useqraft.

qr_code_logo.title: QR Code with Logo
qr_code_logo.meta_title: How to Add a Logo to a QR Code | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_logo.meta_description: Add a logo to the center of your QR code using high error correction. Learn size limits and best practices.
qr_code_logo.definition: Adding a logo to a QR code means overlaying an image (typically your brand logo) in the center of the QR pattern. This is possible thanks to error correction which compensates for the obscured modules.
qr_code_logo.how_it_works: The logo covers some of the QR code's data modules. With error correction level H (30%), up to 30% of the code can be obscured and still scan correctly. The logo should cover no more than 20-25% of the total area to leave a safety margin. Use a simple, high-contrast logo for best results.

example_1.title: Brand recognition
example_1.description: Add your company logo to make QR codes instantly recognizable.

example_2.title: Marketing materials
example_2.description: Branded QR codes on flyers and posters look more professional and trustworthy.

example_3.title: Product packaging
example_3.description: Integrate your brand identity into functional QR codes on packaging.

qr_code_logo.faq_q1: How big should the logo be?
qr_code_logo.faq_a1: The logo should cover no more than 20-25% of the QR code area. useqraft handles this automatically.
qr_code_logo.faq_a2: PNG with transparent background. Keep it simple — detailed logos become unreadable at small sizes.
qr_code_logo.faq_q3: Does adding a logo affect scannability?
qr_code_logo.faq_a3: It can if the logo is too large or error correction is too low. Always use error correction H when adding a logo.

qr_code_colors.title: QR Code Colors
qr_code_colors.meta_title: QR Code Color Customization Guide | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_colors.meta_description: Customize QR code colors while maintaining scannability. The key rule: dark foreground on light background.
qr_code_colors.definition: QR code color customization lets you change the foreground (modules) and background colors. The critical rule is maintaining sufficient contrast between the two for scanners to read the code.
qr_code_colors.how_it_works: QR scanners detect the contrast between dark modules and light background. The foreground must always be darker than the background. Avoid low-contrast combinations (yellow on white, light gray on white). Test on multiple devices after customizing.

example_1.title: Brand colors
example_1.description: Match your QR code to your brand palette while keeping contrast high.

example_2.title: Event theming
example_2.description: Color-coordinate QR codes with event materials and invitations.

example_3.title: Print integration
example_3.description: Blend QR codes into poster designs using complementary colors.

qr_code_colors.faq_a1: Yes, as long as the foreground is darker than the background. Avoid very light foregrounds or very dark backgrounds.
qr_code_colors.faq_a2: Some generators support gradients, but solid colors are safer for scannability.
qr_code_colors.faq_a3: Black on white has the highest contrast. For branded codes, use a dark version of your brand color on white or very light background.

svg_vs_png_qr.title: SVG vs PNG for QR Codes
svg_vs_png_qr.meta_title: SVG vs PNG QR Code: Which Format to Use? | useqraft Glossary
svg_vs_png_qr.meta_description: SVG scales infinitely for print. PNG is universal for digital. Choose the right QR code export format.
svg_vs_png_qr.definition: SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) and PNG (Portable Network Graphics) are the two main export formats for QR codes. SVG is vector-based and scales infinitely; PNG is raster-based with fixed resolution.
svg_vs_png_qr.how_it_works: SVG defines the QR code as mathematical shapes (rectangles). It can be scaled to any size without quality loss, making it ideal for print. PNG stores the QR code as a pixel grid. Quality degrades when enlarged, but it's universally supported by all devices and platforms.

example_1.title: Print materials
example_1.description: Use SVG for business cards, posters, and packaging — perfect quality at any size.

example_2.title: Website or email
example_2.description: Use PNG for embedding in web pages, emails, and social media posts.

example_3.title: Large format
example_3.description: SVG is essential for billboards and banners where massive enlargement is needed.

svg_vs_png_qr.faq_q1: Which format is better for print?
svg_vs_png_qr.faq_a1: SVG. It scales infinitely without pixelation, ensuring crisp output at any print size.
svg_vs_png_qr.faq_q2: Can I convert PNG to SVG?
svg_vs_png_qr.faq_a2: Not reliably. Raster-to-vector conversion loses precision. Generate the QR code directly in SVG format.
svg_vs_png_qr.faq_a3: Yes. useqraft lets you export QR codes in PNG, SVG, JPEG, and PDF.

batch_qr_codes.title: Batch QR Code Generation
batch_qr_codes.meta_title: Batch QR Code Generation: Create Hundreds at Once | useqraft Glossary
batch_qr_codes.meta_description: Generate hundreds of QR codes at once with batch creation. Upload a CSV, customize, and export as ZIP.
batch_qr_codes.definition: Batch QR code generation allows you to create multiple QR codes simultaneously from a list of data (URLs, contacts, WiFi credentials). Results are exported as a ZIP archive.
batch_qr_codes.how_it_works: Upload a CSV or spreadsheet with one row per QR code. The generator processes each row, applies your chosen style (colors, logo, error correction), and produces individual QR code images. Download all of them in a single ZIP file.

example_1.title: Product labels
example_1.description: Generate unique QR codes for each product SKU from an inventory spreadsheet.

example_2.title: Event badges
example_2.description: Create personalized QR codes for each attendee from a registration list.

example_3.title: Multi-location business
example_3.description: Generate Google Reviews QR codes for each branch location.

batch_qr_codes.faq_q1: How many QR codes can I create at once?
batch_qr_codes.faq_a1: On useqraft Pro, you can generate up to 500 QR codes in a single batch.
batch_qr_codes.faq_q2: What file format do I upload?
batch_qr_codes.faq_a2: CSV files with one column per QR code field (URL, name, etc.).
batch_qr_codes.faq_q3: Can I customize each QR code individually?
batch_qr_codes.faq_a3: Batch applies the same style to all codes. For individual customization, create codes one by one.

qr_code_api.title: QR Code API
qr_code_api.meta_title: QR Code API: Generate Codes Programmatically | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_api.meta_description: Use a QR code API to generate, customize, and track QR codes programmatically from your applications.
qr_code_api.definition: A QR code API lets developers generate, customize, and manage QR codes programmatically through HTTP requests. This enables integration into apps, websites, and automated workflows.
qr_code_api.how_it_works: Send a POST request with parameters (content, type, colors, size, error correction) to the API endpoint. The server generates the QR code and returns it as an image (PNG/SVG) or a JSON response with a download URL. API keys authenticate requests and enforce rate limits.

example_1.title: E-commerce platform
example_1.description: Auto-generate product QR codes when new items are added to inventory.

example_2.title: Ticketing system
example_2.description: Generate unique QR codes for each ticket purchase in real-time.

example_3.title: CRM integration
example_3.description: Auto-create vCard QR codes for new contacts in your CRM.

qr_code_api.faq_a1: Yes. useqraft Pro includes API access with endpoints for creating, reading, and managing QR codes.
qr_code_api.faq_a2: Check the useqraft API documentation for current rate limits. Pro plans offer higher throughput.
qr_code_api.faq_q3: Can I track scans via the API?
qr_code_api.faq_a3: Yes. The API includes endpoints for retrieving scan analytics data.

short_url_redirect.title: Short URL Redirect
short_url_redirect.meta_title: How QR Code Short URL Redirects Work | useqraft Glossary
short_url_redirect.meta_description: Dynamic QR codes use short URL redirects to enable tracking and editable destinations.
short_url_redirect.definition: A short URL redirect is the mechanism behind dynamic QR codes. Instead of encoding the full destination URL, the QR code contains a short URL that redirects to the final destination through a server.
short_url_redirect.how_it_works: The QR code encodes a compact URL (e.g., useqraft.com/r/abc123). When scanned, the browser hits this URL, the server looks up the destination in its database, logs the scan, and issues an HTTP 302 redirect to the actual page. This keeps the QR code simple while enabling tracking and URL changes.

example_1.title: Editable destinations
example_1.description: Change where the QR code points without reprinting.

example_2.title: Scan tracking
example_2.description: The redirect server logs each scan with location and device data.

example_3.title: Shorter QR codes
example_3.description: Short URLs produce simpler, easier-to-scan QR patterns.

short_url_redirect.faq_q1: Does the redirect slow down loading?
short_url_redirect.faq_a1: The redirect adds ~50-100ms. Users don't notice the difference.
short_url_redirect.faq_q2: What happens if the redirect server goes down?
short_url_redirect.faq_a2: The QR code stops working. That's why choosing a reliable provider like useqraft matters.
short_url_redirect.faq_q3: Can I use my own domain for the short URL?
short_url_redirect.faq_a3: Some enterprise plans support custom domains. useqraft uses useqraft.com/r/ by default.

utm_tracking_qr.title: UTM Tracking with QR Codes
utm_tracking_qr.meta_title: UTM Parameters for QR Codes | useqraft Glossary
utm_tracking_qr.meta_description: Add UTM parameters to QR code URLs to track campaigns in Google Analytics.
utm_tracking_qr.definition: UTM tracking combines QR codes with UTM parameters (source, medium, campaign) appended to the destination URL, enabling precise campaign tracking in Google Analytics.
utm_tracking_qr.how_it_works: Append UTM parameters to your destination URL before creating the QR code: ?utm_source=qr&utm_medium=print&utm_campaign=spring2026. When users land on your page, Google Analytics captures these parameters and attributes the visit to your QR campaign.

example_1.title: Print campaign tracking
example_1.description: Track scans from specific flyers, posters, or direct mail pieces.

example_2.title: Multi-channel comparison
example_2.description: Compare QR code performance against email and social campaigns.

example_3.title: Store-level tracking
example_3.description: Use unique campaign names per store location to compare performance.

utm_tracking_qr.faq_q1: Which UTM parameters should I use?
utm_tracking_qr.faq_q2: Do UTM parameters make the QR code more complex?
utm_tracking_qr.faq_a2: Yes. Longer URLs create denser QR codes. Use a dynamic QR code to keep the encoded URL short.
utm_tracking_qr.faq_q3: Can I use UTM tracking with static QR codes?
utm_tracking_qr.faq_a3: Yes, but the UTM parameters are baked into the QR code and can't be changed later.

qr_code_security.title: QR Code Security
qr_code_security.meta_title: QR Code Security Risks and Best Practices | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_security.meta_description: QR codes can be exploited for phishing. Learn security best practices to protect your users.
qr_code_security.definition: QR code security covers the risks and best practices around using QR codes safely. Since QR codes can encode any URL, they can potentially direct users to malicious websites.
qr_code_security.how_it_works: The main risk is 'QR phishing' (quishing): attackers place malicious QR codes over legitimate ones, redirecting users to phishing sites. Best practices include: using a trusted generator, previewing URLs before visiting, using dynamic codes (the provider validates the destination), and placing QR codes in tamper-evident locations.

example_1.title: Payment fraud prevention
example_1.description: Use tamper-evident stickers for payment QR codes in stores.

example_2.title: Corporate security
example_2.description: Educate employees to preview QR code URLs before clicking.

example_3.title: Event safety
example_3.description: Use branded, recognizable QR codes that attendees can trust.

qr_code_security.faq_a1: QR codes themselves are just data. The risk is in what they link to. Always preview the URL before visiting.
qr_code_security.faq_q2: How do I protect my QR codes from tampering?
qr_code_security.faq_a2: Use tamper-evident materials, place codes in monitored areas, and use dynamic codes so you can verify the destination.
qr_code_security.faq_q3: Is it safe to scan QR codes in public?
qr_code_security.faq_a3: Be cautious. Preview the URL, check for stickers placed over original codes, and avoid codes from unknown sources.

nfc_vs_qr.title: NFC vs QR Code
nfc_vs_qr.meta_title: NFC vs QR Code: Which Technology to Choose? | useqraft Glossary
nfc_vs_qr.meta_description: NFC requires close contact and special tags. QR codes work at distance with any camera. Compare both technologies.
nfc_vs_qr.definition: NFC (Near Field Communication) and QR codes are both contactless technologies for sharing data. NFC requires physical proximity (~4cm) and a compatible device, while QR codes work at any distance with a camera.
nfc_vs_qr.how_it_works: NFC uses radio waves between a tag and a reader device. The user taps their phone on the NFC tag to receive data. QR codes use a visual pattern scanned by a camera. NFC tags cost $0.10-2 each and need physical installation; QR codes are free to generate and can be printed anywhere.

example_1.title: Cost comparison
example_1.description: QR codes are free to create and print. NFC tags cost money per unit.

example_2.title: Range
example_2.description: QR codes work from centimeters to meters. NFC requires contact.

example_3.title: Durability
example_3.description: NFC tags can be embedded in materials. QR codes can be damaged by wear.

nfc_vs_qr.faq_a1: QR codes. They work with all smartphones, cost nothing to create, and can be printed on any material.
nfc_vs_qr.faq_a2: Yes. Many businesses combine NFC tags with QR codes as a fallback for devices without NFC.
nfc_vs_qr.faq_a3: Most modern smartphones do, but older devices and some budget phones lack NFC. QR code support is universal.

mecard_format.title: MeCard Format
mecard_format.meta_title: MeCard vs vCard: Contact QR Code Formats | useqraft Glossary
mecard_format.meta_description: MeCard is a compact contact format used by some QR generators. Compare with vCard for compatibility.
mecard_format.definition: MeCard is a compact contact data format developed by NTT DoCoMo. It encodes name, phone, email, and address in a shorter syntax than vCard, resulting in simpler QR codes.
mecard_format.how_it_works: MeCard uses a compact syntax: MECARD:N:Doe,John;TEL:+123456;EMAIL:john@example.com;;. It's shorter than vCard format, producing a less dense QR code. However, vCard is more widely supported and allows more fields (company, title, website, notes).

example_1.title: Simple contact sharing
example_1.description: Use MeCard when you only need name, phone, and email — the QR code is simpler.

example_2.title: Japanese market
example_2.description: MeCard originated in Japan and is widely supported by Japanese phones.

example_3.title: Small print
example_3.description: The compact format allows for smaller QR codes on space-limited materials.

mecard_format.faq_q1: Should I use MeCard or vCard?
mecard_format.faq_a1: vCard is recommended for most cases — it's more widely supported and allows more fields.
mecard_format.faq_a2: useqraft uses vCard format as it offers broader compatibility and richer contact information.
mecard_format.faq_a3: Most modern phones support both, but vCard has near-universal support.

epc_qr_code.title: EPC QR Code
epc_qr_code.meta_title: What is an EPC QR Code? (Payment QR) | useqraft Glossary
epc_qr_code.meta_description: EPC QR codes encode SEPA payment data for instant bank transfers in Europe. Learn how they work.
epc_qr_code.definition: An EPC (European Payments Council) QR code encodes SEPA bank transfer data: IBAN, amount, recipient name, and payment reference. Scanning it pre-fills a bank transfer in the user's banking app.
epc_qr_code.how_it_works: The QR code follows the EPC069-12 standard, encoding structured payment data. When scanned with a banking app, all transfer fields are pre-filled: recipient IBAN, amount, name, and reference. The user just confirms the payment. Widely used for invoices and donations in SEPA countries.

example_1.title: Invoices
example_1.description: Add an EPC QR code to invoices for one-scan payment.

example_2.title: Donations
example_2.description: Nonprofits display EPC QR codes for instant donation transfers.

example_3.title: Rent payments
example_3.description: Landlords provide EPC QR codes for monthly rent transfers.

epc_qr_code.faq_a1: EPC QR codes are designed for SEPA transfers and work primarily in the Eurozone.
epc_qr_code.faq_a2: Yes. The QR code only pre-fills transfer details. The user must authenticate and confirm in their banking app.
epc_qr_code.faq_a3: Yes. You can encode a fixed amount or leave it blank for the user to enter.

qr_code_version.title: QR Code Version
qr_code_version.meta_title: QR Code Versions 1-40 Explained | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_version.meta_description: QR codes come in 40 versions, from 21x21 to 177x177 modules. Higher versions store more data.
qr_code_version.definition: QR code version refers to the size of the QR code matrix, ranging from Version 1 (21x21 modules) to Version 40 (177x177 modules). Higher versions can store more data but produce denser patterns.
qr_code_version.how_it_works: Each version increases the matrix by 4 modules per side. Version 1 (21x21) stores up to 17 characters. Version 40 (177x177) stores up to 4,296 characters. The generator automatically selects the smallest version that fits your data and error correction level.

example_1.title: Short URL
example_1.description: A simple URL uses Version 2-4, producing a clean, easy-to-scan code.

example_2.title: vCard contact
example_2.description: Full contact details may require Version 10+, creating a denser pattern.

example_3.title: Long text
example_3.description: Paragraphs of text push to higher versions, requiring larger print sizes.

qr_code_version.faq_q1: Can I choose the QR code version?
qr_code_version.faq_a1: Most generators auto-select the smallest version for your data. Keep content short for simpler codes.
qr_code_version.faq_q2: What version do most QR codes use?
qr_code_version.faq_a2: Versions 2-6 cover the vast majority of use cases (URLs, short text, WiFi).
qr_code_version.faq_a3: Higher versions produce denser patterns that need larger print sizes and closer scanning distance.

micro_qr_code.title: Micro QR Code
micro_qr_code.meta_title: What is a Micro QR Code? | useqraft Glossary
micro_qr_code.meta_description: Micro QR codes are smaller variants that store less data. Used in manufacturing where space is extremely limited.
micro_qr_code.definition: Micro QR code is a compact variant of the standard QR code. It uses only one position detection pattern (instead of three) and can store up to 35 numeric characters.
micro_qr_code.how_it_works: By removing two of the three finder patterns and simplifying the format, Micro QR codes require significantly less space. They come in 4 versions (M1-M4) with matrices from 11x11 to 17x17 modules. The trade-off is limited data capacity and less error correction.

example_1.title: PCB marking
example_1.description: Mark electronic components with tiny codes containing serial numbers.

example_2.title: Medical devices
example_2.description: Label small medical instruments with identification codes.

example_3.title: Jewelry tags
example_3.description: Encode product IDs on tiny labels attached to jewelry.

micro_qr_code.faq_q1: Can phones scan Micro QR codes?
micro_qr_code.faq_a1: Support varies. Most standard QR readers don't support Micro QR. Specialized scanners or apps are needed.
micro_qr_code.faq_q2: When should I use Micro QR?
micro_qr_code.faq_a2: Only when physical space is extremely limited and data is very short (serial numbers, IDs).
micro_qr_code.faq_q3: Does useqraft generate Micro QR codes?
micro_qr_code.faq_a3: No. Standard QR codes are universally supported and recommended for all consumer-facing use cases.

qr_code_encoding.title: QR Code Encoding
qr_code_encoding.meta_title: QR Code Encoding Modes Explained | useqraft Glossary
qr_code_encoding.meta_description: QR codes support 4 encoding modes: numeric, alphanumeric, byte, and kanji. Each optimizes for different data types.
qr_code_encoding.definition: QR code encoding refers to how data is converted into the QR code's binary pattern. Four encoding modes exist: Numeric (digits only), Alphanumeric (digits + uppercase + symbols), Byte (any character), and Kanji (Japanese characters).
qr_code_encoding.how_it_works: The encoder selects the most efficient mode for your data. Numeric mode packs 3 digits per 10 bits. Alphanumeric packs 2 characters per 11 bits. Byte mode uses 8 bits per character. Using the optimal mode minimizes the QR code size. The generator handles this automatically.

example_1.title: Phone numbers
example_1.description: Pure numeric data uses the most efficient encoding, producing the smallest QR code.

example_2.title: URLs
example_2.description: URLs use byte mode due to lowercase letters and special characters.

example_3.title: Mixed content
example_3.description: The encoder can switch modes within a single QR code to optimize space.

qr_code_encoding.faq_q1: Do I need to choose the encoding mode?
qr_code_encoding.faq_a1: No. QR generators automatically select the optimal mode for your data.
qr_code_encoding.faq_a2: Indirectly — more efficient encoding means a simpler QR code that's easier to scan.
qr_code_encoding.faq_q3: Can QR codes encode binary data?
qr_code_encoding.faq_a3: Yes. Byte mode can encode any binary data, though it's less space-efficient than specialized modes.

complete_guide.title: The Complete QR Code Guide 2026
complete_guide.meta_title: The Complete QR Code Guide 2026 | useqraft
complete_guide.meta_description: Everything you need to know about QR codes: how they work, types, creation, customization, analytics, and best practices.
complete_guide.intro: QR codes have evolved from a niche manufacturing tool to a universal bridge between physical and digital worlds. This guide covers everything from basic concepts to advanced strategies for businesses and creators.
complete_guide.section_1_content: A QR (Quick Response) code is a two-dimensional barcode that stores data in a matrix of black and white squares. Invented in 1994 by Denso Wave for tracking automotive parts, QR codes can now store URLs, text, contact information, WiFi credentials, and more. Unlike traditional barcodes that encode data in one direction, QR codes use both horizontal and vertical axes, allowing them to store up to 4,296 alphanumeric characters.
complete_guide.section_2_content: A QR code consists of several key components: three finder patterns (large squares in corners) for orientation, alignment patterns for distortion correction, timing patterns that establish the grid, and data modules that encode your content. When you point a camera at a QR code, the software locates the finder patterns, determines the code's orientation and size, reads the data modules, applies error correction to fix any unreadable modules, and decodes the content.
complete_guide.section_3_content: Static QR codes embed data directly into the pattern. They never expire and work offline, but the content cannot be changed after creation. Dynamic QR codes encode a short redirect URL instead. This allows you to change the destination without reprinting, track scan analytics (location, device, time), and keep the QR code simpler because the encoded URL is shorter. For most business use cases, dynamic QR codes are the better choice.
complete_guide.section_4_content: QR codes can encode many data types: URL (website links), plain text, WiFi credentials (auto-connect to networks), vCard (save contacts to phone), email (pre-filled mailto links), phone numbers, SMS, WhatsApp messages, calendar events, geographic locations, social media profiles, PDF documents, cryptocurrency addresses, and app store links. Each type uses a specific encoding format recognized by smartphones.
complete_guide.section_5_title: How to create a QR code
complete_guide.section_5_content: Creating a QR code with useqraft takes seconds: choose the QR type (URL, WiFi, vCard, etc.), enter your content, customize the appearance (colors, logo, error correction), and export in your preferred format (PNG, SVG, PDF). For dynamic codes, sign up for a free account to enable tracking and editable destinations. Batch creation is available for generating hundreds of codes from a CSV file.
complete_guide.section_6_content: Modern QR codes don't have to be black and white. You can customize the foreground and background colors (maintaining dark-on-light contrast), add your brand logo in the center (using high error correction to compensate), adjust the size and error correction level, and export in vector format (SVG) for perfect print quality. The key rule: always test your customized QR code on multiple devices before printing.
complete_guide.section_7_content: Dynamic QR codes provide scan analytics: total scan count, geographic distribution (city-level), device and browser breakdown, operating system statistics, and time-based trends. This data helps measure campaign ROI, understand audience demographics, optimize placement and timing, and compare performance across different materials or locations. useqraft displays these analytics in an intuitive dashboard with charts and a world map.
complete_guide.section_8_content: Follow these proven practices for effective QR codes: always add a call to action near the code ('Scan to see menu'), ensure sufficient size (minimum 2cm for close range), test on multiple devices before printing, use high error correction (H) when adding logos, keep encoded data short for simpler patterns, use dynamic codes for anything you might need to update, place codes where they are easy to scan (eye level, good lighting), and always link to mobile-optimized content.
complete_guide.faq_q1: Are QR codes free to create?
complete_guide.faq_a1: Yes. useqraft lets you create unlimited static QR codes for free. Dynamic QR codes with tracking are available on the free plan (5 codes) and unlimited on Pro.
complete_guide.faq_q2: Do QR codes work without internet?
complete_guide.faq_a2: Static QR codes with text or WiFi data work offline. QR codes linking to URLs require internet to load the destination page.
complete_guide.faq_q3: How long do QR codes last?
complete_guide.faq_a3: Static codes last forever. Dynamic codes on useqraft free plan last 30 days. Pro dynamic codes are permanent.
complete_guide.faq_q4: Can QR codes be copied or counterfeited?
complete_guide.faq_a4: Anyone can reproduce a QR code by scanning and reprinting it. For secure applications, use dynamic codes with tracking to detect unusual scan patterns.
complete_guide.faq_q5: What's the maximum data a QR code can store?
complete_guide.faq_a5: Up to 4,296 alphanumeric characters or 7,089 numeric characters. However, shorter data produces simpler, easier-to-scan codes.

dynamic_vs_static.title: Dynamic vs Static QR Codes: The Definitive Guide
dynamic_vs_static.meta_title: Dynamic vs Static QR Codes: Complete Comparison | useqraft
dynamic_vs_static.meta_description: Understand the key differences between dynamic and static QR codes. Learn when to use each type for your business.
dynamic_vs_static.intro: Choosing between dynamic and static QR codes is the most important decision when creating QR codes for your business. This guide explains the fundamental differences, use cases, and helps you make the right choice.
dynamic_vs_static.section_1_title: What is a static QR code?
dynamic_vs_static.section_1_content: A static QR code encodes your data directly into its pattern of modules. The URL, text, WiFi credentials, or contact information is embedded permanently into the QR code itself. Once created, a static code cannot be modified — the pattern is fixed. Static codes work without any server or internet connection (for non-URL data), never expire, and are completely free to generate. The trade-off: no tracking, no editing, and longer content creates denser, harder-to-scan patterns.
dynamic_vs_static.section_2_title: What is a dynamic QR code?
dynamic_vs_static.section_2_content: A dynamic QR code encodes a short redirect URL (like useqraft.com/r/abc123) instead of your actual content. When scanned, the user's device hits the redirect server, which logs the scan and forwards to the real destination. This architecture enables powerful features: change the destination URL anytime without reprinting, track every scan with location, device, and time data, keep the QR code pattern simple regardless of actual content length, and set expiration dates for time-limited campaigns.
dynamic_vs_static.section_3_content: Data storage: static embeds directly, dynamic uses redirect. Editable: static no, dynamic yes. Scan tracking: static no, dynamic yes. Works offline: static yes (non-URL), dynamic no. Expiration: static never, dynamic configurable. Pattern complexity: static depends on data length, dynamic always simple. Cost: static free, dynamic may require a plan. Best for: static permanent fixtures, dynamic marketing campaigns.
dynamic_vs_static.section_4_title: When to use static QR codes
dynamic_vs_static.section_4_content: Choose static QR codes when: the content will never change (WiFi password, fixed URL, contact card), you don't need scan tracking, the QR code must work offline (WiFi, vCard), you're printing on permanent fixtures (plaques, monuments, product labels), or cost is the primary concern. Common static use cases include WiFi sharing, business cards with vCard, product packaging with fixed URLs, and equipment labels with serial numbers.
dynamic_vs_static.section_5_title: When to use dynamic QR codes
dynamic_vs_static.section_5_content: Choose dynamic QR codes when: you might need to update the destination, tracking scan data is valuable, the original URL is long (the redirect keeps the QR simple), you're running time-limited campaigns, or you want to A/B test destinations. Common dynamic use cases include marketing campaigns, restaurant menus that change, event promotions with changing details, and any scenario where you want analytics on scan behavior.
dynamic_vs_static.section_6_content: useqraft makes it easy to create both types. For static codes: choose your QR type, enter content, customize design, and export — no account needed. For dynamic codes: sign up for a free account, create the code, customize, and export. You'll get a dashboard to track scans, change destinations, and manage expiration. The free plan includes 5 dynamic codes with basic analytics. Pro unlocks unlimited codes with full analytics including geographic map, device breakdown, and timeline charts.
dynamic_vs_static.faq_q1: Can I convert a static QR code to dynamic?
dynamic_vs_static.faq_a1: No. The encoding is fundamentally different. You need to create a new dynamic QR code and replace the printed one.
dynamic_vs_static.faq_q2: Are dynamic QR codes less reliable?
dynamic_vs_static.faq_a2: They depend on the redirect server being online. Choose a reliable provider. useqraft guarantees 99.9% uptime.
dynamic_vs_static.faq_q3: Do dynamic QR codes load slower?
dynamic_vs_static.faq_a3: The redirect adds ~50-100ms, which is imperceptible. The user experience is identical.
dynamic_vs_static.faq_a4: Dynamic codes can be monitored for unusual activity and disabled if compromised. Static codes, once printed, cannot be controlled.
dynamic_vs_static.faq_q5: Can I use both types in the same campaign?
dynamic_vs_static.faq_a5: Absolutely. Use static for permanent elements (WiFi, contact cards) and dynamic for trackable campaign links.

business_guide.title: QR Codes for Business: The Strategic Guide
business_guide.meta_title: QR Codes for Business: Strategy & ROI Guide | useqraft
business_guide.meta_description: Learn how to use QR codes strategically for marketing, operations, and customer engagement. Includes ROI measurement and implementation roadmap.
business_guide.intro: QR codes are one of the most cost-effective tools for bridging offline and online customer touchpoints. This guide goes beyond basic usage to cover strategic implementation, ROI measurement, and scaling with batch creation and APIs.
business_guide.section_1_title: Why businesses need QR codes in 2026
business_guide.section_1_content: Consumer smartphone usage is above 90% in most markets. QR code scanning has become second nature since the pandemic. Businesses that don't offer QR touchpoints miss opportunities for frictionless customer engagement, measurable offline-to-online conversion, cost-effective marketing with zero marginal cost per interaction, and data collection without privacy concerns (anonymous scan analytics).
business_guide.section_2_content: In marketing, QR codes bridge print and digital. Place them on business cards linking to your portfolio, product packaging linking to instruction videos or reviews, posters and flyers with trackable campaign URLs, direct mail with personalized landing pages, and storefronts linking to Google Reviews. Use UTM parameters to track campaign performance in Google Analytics alongside your digital channels.
business_guide.section_3_content: Beyond marketing, QR codes streamline operations: inventory management with unique QR codes per SKU, equipment maintenance logs (scan to view service history), employee onboarding (QR links to training materials), facility management (room booking, supply requests), and document access (scan to view latest version of a policy or manual). These internal use cases often deliver the highest ROI through time savings.
business_guide.section_4_content: QR codes create instant touchpoints: restaurant menus (reduce printing costs, update in real-time), WiFi access (no more password cards), loyalty programs (scan to earn points), feedback collection (scan to leave a review), and event check-in (scan ticket QR for instant entry). Each interaction is an opportunity to deliver value and collect anonymous analytics.
business_guide.section_5_content: Dynamic QR codes provide the data you need to measure return on investment: scan volume tells you reach, geographic data shows where your audience is, device breakdown reveals whether to optimize for iOS or Android, time trends identify peak engagement periods, and campaign comparison shows which materials perform best. Calculate ROI by comparing QR campaign cost (near zero) to the value of conversions driven by scans.
business_guide.section_6_content: Scaling QR codes across a business requires automation. Batch creation lets you generate hundreds of codes from a CSV file — perfect for multi-location businesses, product lines, or event attendee lists. The useqraft API enables programmatic QR code generation, allowing integration with your CRM, e-commerce platform, ticketing system, or any custom workflow. Pro plans include full API access with generous rate limits.
business_guide.section_7_content: Start small and scale: Phase 1 — pick one high-impact use case (Google Reviews, WiFi sharing, or menu). Phase 2 — create dynamic codes and track results for 30 days. Phase 3 — analyze scan data and optimize placement, design, and call-to-action. Phase 4 — expand to additional use cases based on what you learned. Phase 5 — integrate API for automation if volume justifies it. This incremental approach minimizes risk and builds organizational buy-in.
business_guide.faq_q1: How much do business QR codes cost?
business_guide.faq_a1: useqraft free plan covers 5 dynamic codes. Pro at 9.99 euros per month gives unlimited codes, full analytics, batch export, and API access.
business_guide.faq_q2: Do customers actually scan QR codes?
business_guide.faq_a2: Yes. Post-pandemic, QR code usage has become mainstream. The key is providing clear value and a visible call-to-action.
business_guide.faq_q3: Can I brand QR codes with my company colors?
business_guide.faq_a3: Absolutely. useqraft lets you customize foreground and background colors, add your logo, and choose from templates.
business_guide.faq_q4: What if my business has multiple locations?
business_guide.faq_a4: Use batch creation to generate unique codes per location. Track each location's performance separately in the analytics dashboard.
business_guide.faq_q5: Is QR code data GDPR compliant?
business_guide.faq_a5: useqraft scan analytics are fully anonymous — we record city-level location and device type, not personal data. No cookies are used for tracking.

design_guide.title: How to Customize QR Code Design Without Losing Readability
design_guide.meta_title: QR Code Design Guide: Colors, Logo & Readability | useqraft
design_guide.meta_description: Learn how to customize QR code colors, add logos, choose error correction, and pick the right size and export format.
design_guide.intro: A well-designed QR code strengthens your brand while remaining perfectly scannable. This guide covers the rules and techniques for customizing QR codes without compromising readability.
design_guide.section_1_content: A plain black-and-white QR code works, but a branded one performs better. Studies show branded QR codes get 30% more scans than generic ones. Design signals trust (this is from a real business), reinforces brand recognition, and integrates naturally into marketing materials. The goal is to customize enough to stand out while respecting the technical constraints that keep the code scannable.
design_guide.section_2_content: The fundamental rule: the foreground (modules) must be darker than the background. QR scanners rely on contrast between dark and light areas. Safe choices include dark brand color on white, dark brand color on very light tint, and black on light brand color. Avoid light foreground on dark background (inverted), similar-brightness colors (gray on gray), and yellow or light green foreground on white. Always test on at least 3 different devices after customizing colors.
design_guide.section_3_content: Place your logo in the center of the QR code, covering no more than 20-25% of the total area. Set error correction to H (30%) to compensate for the obscured modules. Logo best practices: use a simple, recognizable mark (not your full wordmark), export the logo as PNG with transparent background, keep it high contrast against the QR pattern, and avoid logos with fine details that become unreadable at small sizes. useqraft automatically positions and sizes the logo for optimal scannability.
design_guide.section_4_content: QR codes support four error correction levels: L (7% recovery) for maximum data capacity with minimal damage tolerance, M (15% recovery) the default balance for most use cases, Q (25% recovery) for codes exposed to moderate wear, and H (30% recovery) required when adding logos or for harsh environments. Higher error correction means a denser pattern, so the printed size needs to be larger. Use M for digital displays, Q for standard print, and H for logo overlays and outdoor signage.
design_guide.section_5_content: The scanning rule of thumb: QR code size should be at least one-tenth of the scanning distance. For a phone at 30cm, minimum 3cm. For a poster at 1m, minimum 10cm. For a billboard at 5m, minimum 50cm. Resolution matters too: use 300 DPI minimum for print. For large format, use SVG (vector) to avoid any pixelation. Digital displays need only 72-150 DPI. Always add a quiet zone (white border) of at least 4 modules around the QR code.
design_guide.section_6_content: Choose your export format based on the use case. SVG (vector): infinite scalability, perfect for print at any size, small file size, editable in design tools. PNG (raster): universal compatibility, best for web and email, choose 300+ DPI for print. PDF: ideal for direct printing, embeds the QR code with fonts and layout, good for invoices and documents. JPEG: acceptable for web but PNG is preferred due to lossless compression (JPEG artifacts can affect scanning). useqraft supports all four formats.
design_guide.faq_q1: Can I use my brand's exact colors?
design_guide.faq_a1: Yes, as long as the foreground is significantly darker than the background. Enter exact hex codes in useqraft's color picker.
design_guide.faq_q2: Will adding a logo make my QR code unscannable?
design_guide.faq_a2: Not if you use error correction H and keep the logo under 25% of the area. useqraft handles this automatically.
design_guide.faq_q3: What's the best format for social media?
design_guide.faq_a3: PNG at 1080x1080 pixels. It's universally supported and maintains quality after platform compression.
design_guide.faq_q4: Can I animate a QR code?
design_guide.faq_a4: Animated QR codes (GIFs) exist but are unreliable for scanning. Stick to static images for reliable results.
design_guide.faq_q5: How do I test my QR code design?
design_guide.faq_a5: Scan with at least 3 different phones (iOS and Android), at the intended distance, in the intended lighting conditions. Test both the digital version and a print sample.
